scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The work reflects the religious and civic norms of 5th-century BCE Athens, specifically regarding the gender-segregated Thesmophoria festival.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play Thesmophoriazusae was first performed in 411 BCE, reflecting contemporary Athenian social and religious laws.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The play satirizes the strict gender boundaries of the Athenian Thesmophoria, a state-recognized festival.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play's debut in 411 BCE provides a contemporary anchor for the described gender-based exclusions in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The context is the Athenian 'mysteries' of the Thesmophoria, which were strictly guarded from male ears.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule reflects contemporary Athenian religious practice at the time of the play's production in 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the specific rights of 'frank speech' (parrhesia) within the internal assembly of Athenian women during the festival.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The work was produced in 411 BCE, documenting the normative expectations of behavior within contemporary Athenian female spaces.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The reference to 'Prytanes' identifies the political administration of the Athenian democracy overseeing public order.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play was performed in 411 BCE during a period of significant political upheaval in Athens, yet the magisterial structure remains central.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the domestic confinement typical of the Athenian 'oikos' (household) for citizen women.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play reflects the social norms of 5th-century BCE Athens, specifically around the time of its 411 BCE production.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Senate' (Boule) was a central institution of the Athenian democratic state, exclusively restricted to male citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristophanes writes for an Athenian audience in 411 BCE, where the exclusion of women from the Boule was absolute.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The festivals mentioned (Sthenia, Scirophoria) are specific religious events of the Athenian civic calendar.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Produced in 411 BCE, the play discusses the allocation of civic honors according to contemporary Athenian values.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mentions of trierarchs and pilots link the rule to the naval and social hierarchy of 5th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: In 411 BCE, during the Peloponnesian War, military performance was the primary metric for social status in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The use of the 'Senate' (Boule) to order public shaming is a feature of Athenian legal procedure.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary judicial and punitive powers of the state in 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Prytanes were the executive leaders of the Athenian Council of 500.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This administrative structure was the standard operating procedure for the Athenian democracy at the time of the play's performance.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Thesmophoria was a pan-Hellenic festival, but the specific legal and grove constraints here refer to the Athenian iteration.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the year of the play's premiere, reflecting contemporary religious law.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the sacred topography and exclusionary laws of the city of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the actual sacred boundaries maintained in Athens during 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Scythian archers were a well-documented slave police force used by the Athenian democratic state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Captures the reality of Athenian policing during the late 5th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of slaves from specific citizen-only religious festivals was a legal norm in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The note refers to the context of the 411 BCE production in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Weaving was a primary domestic duty for free women in the Athenian oikos during the 5th century.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristophanes represents the division of labor in his own lifetime and city.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The trierarchy was a specific Athenian liturgy (public service) imposed on the wealthy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Athens was heavily reliant on the trierarchy in 411 BCE due to the ongoing naval conflict with Sparta.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Scythian archer corps was a distinctive feature of the Athenian public order system.
